attributes.stem:
A 35-year-old woman presents to the birth suite with a one-hour history of vaginal loss of clear fluid and lower abdominal discomfort. She is currently 32 weeks pregnant with her second child. She can still feel fetal movements. Her medical history is significant for a low-lying placenta identified on her 20-week ultrasound with the placenta posterior and 15mm from the cervix. She has not had any bleeding throughout her pregnancy or prior to her presentation today. She has no other significant medical history and takes no regular medications. Her pregnancy has otherwise been uncomplicated. She has not yet had a GBS swab.

On examination, she appears well. Her vital signs are within normal limits. The cardiotocograph demonstrates fetal accelerations, baseline fetal heart rate of 140bpm, no decelerations, normal variability and regular uterine contractions occurring three times in 10 minutes. Abdomen is soft between palpable uterine contractions. The fetus is cephalic and not engaged. Fundal height is 31cm.

Speculum examination is performed which reveals a long closed cervix with pooling of clear liquor in the posterior fornex. No vaginal bleeding is seen.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?
options: Proceed to emergency caesarean section after a betamethasone injection
Administer magnesium sulfate and betamethasone injections
Administer tocolysis with nifedipine and betamethasone injections
Allow the birth to proceed naturally
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with preterm prelabour rupture of membranes (PPROM) that has progressed to preterm labour (PTL) at 32 weeks gestation. The most appropriate next step in management is to administer tocolysis with nifedipine. Tocolysis is crucial in this scenario as it can temporarily halt or slow the progression of labour, allowing time for corticosteroid administration to improve neonatal outcomes. Although this patient had a low lying placenta at 20 weeks - this is not a contradiction for tocolysis as her presentation is with PPROM not vaginal bleeding. Loading dose of Nifedipine (20mg orally, every 30 minutes for 3 doses) is followed by regular nifedipine( 20mg every 6hrs for the following 48 hours) to allow time for corticosteroid effect. Nifedipine is preferred over salbutamol due to a lower risk of maternal side effects, particularly tachycardia, which can confuse the clinical picture when monitoring for chorioamnionitis.

The administration of betamethasone (corticosteroid) improves fetal outcomes by enhancing lung maturity.

Proceeding to emergency caesarean section is not the most appropriate first step. It is possible that the pregnancy may continue to progress and caesarean at this point may unnecessarily deliver the baby prematurely. Caesarean may be considered in cases where there is fetal distress, malpresentation such as breech or transverse lie with a dilating cervix or concern for vaginal bleeding due to placenta previa.

Administering magnesium sulfate and betamethasone injections is not the most appropriate management at 32 weeks. Magnesium sulfate is used as neuroprotection in very preterm infants (typically less than 30 weeks gestation) to reduce the risk of cerebral palsy. Generally magnesium is not used over 30 weeks as the numbers needed to treat for potential benefit are much greater.

Admitting for surveillance without intervention is insufficient. While admission is necessary, active management with tocolysis and corticosteroids is crucial to improve neonatal outcomes given the palpable regular uterine activity and confirmed ruptured membranes.

Allowing the birth to proceed naturally at 32 weeks gestation is not recommended when there's an opportunity to improve neonatal outcomes through medical intervention.

Following tocolysis, the management should include admission, high vaginal swab and urine for microscopy, culture and sensitivity, ultrasound to confirm cephalic presentation, fetal growth parameters and placental position, and administration of betamethasone (11.4mg intramuscularly) for fetal lung maturation. Antibiotics should also be prescribed to prevent ascending infection, which is a significant risk after membrane rupture. The goal is to delay delivery, if possible, to allow for the full course of corticosteroids and potentially transfer to a facility with appropriate neonatal care capabilities if not already present.
learningPoints: Tocolysis in preterm premature rupture of membranes allows time for corticosteroid administration, improving neonatal outcomes.
Management of preterm labour includes admission, steroids, tocolysis, antibiotics, and preparation for potential preterm delivery.
Nifedipine is preferred over salbutamol for tocolysis due to fewer maternal side effects.
references: Thomson AJ; Royal College of Obstetricians and Gynaecologists. Available at: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/31207667/

Available at: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/11293640/

Caughey AB, Robinson JN, Norwitz ER. Contemporary diagnosis and management of preterm premature rupture of membranes. Rev Obstet Gynecol. 2008 Winter;1(1):11-22. Available at: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/18701929/
